commands.review_summary:
The PR passes Google Chat `isBotSender` into inbound sender facts and adds monitor tests for bot and human sender events.

PR surface: Source +1, Tests +70. Total +71 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main computes `isBotSender` for Google Chat and then omits it from the sender payload that feeds `SenderIsBot` and prompt `is_bot` rendering.
commands.review_followup: - No automated repair is needed; the remaining action is ordinary maintainer review and merge gating.

target.title: fix(googlechat): expose sender bot status in context
target.body:
**Prompt request:** Make the Google Chat channel forward sender bot status into the inbound agent context so an operator running `allowBots` can tell a bot sender from a human, matching what Telegram already exposes, while preserving existing bot detection and filtering.

## What Problem This Solves

Google Chat already detects whether an inbound message was sent by a bot (`sender.type === "BOT"`) and uses that for loop protection, but it never forwards the fact to the agent. With `allowBots: true`, a bot-sent message reaches the agent indistinguishable from a human one: the "Sender (untrusted metadata)" block in the prompt has no `is_bot` field. Operators wiring bot-to-bot or integration flows on Google Chat therefore cannot have the agent treat bot senders differently, even though Telegram exposes this today.

## Why This Change Was Made

The shared inbound context already carries sender bot status end-to-end: `buildChannelInboundEventContext` maps `sender.isBot` to `SenderIsBot`, which renders as `is_bot` in the untrusted sender metadata block. Telegram populates it; Google Chat computes the same signal (`isBotSender`) but does not pass it through. This change forwards that existing flag into the `sender` object Google Chat already builds, so no core or SDK change is needed. This PR only changes the Google Chat adapter's inbound context assembly and its tests.

## User Impact

Operators with `allowBots` enabled now see `"is_bot": true` in the untrusted sender metadata for bot-sent Google Chat messages, letting their agent or system prompt branch on sender type (for example, not auto-replying to other bots). Human messages are unaffected — the field is simply absent. There is no change when `allowBots` is off, where bot messages are still filtered as before.

## Evidence

Direct production-path run via `node --import tsx` — real production functions with no mocked I/O, not a vitest or CI run (Google Chat's inbound access policy needs live pairing/credential infra that is not reproducible offline, so the production core context builder `buildChannelInboundEventContext` and the prompt renderer `buildInboundUserContextPrefix` were invoked directly, using the exact sender expressions the Google Chat monitor builds: `isBotSender = sender.type === "BOT"`, then the `sender` object with `isBot: isBotSender || undefined`), for a bot sender and a human sender. Redacted terminal output of the agent-visible `Sender (untrusted metadata)` block:

~~~text
=== bot sender (sender.type === BOT) ===
sender.isBot (monitor build) = true  ->  ctxPayload.SenderIsBot = true
Sender (untrusted metadata):
{
  "label": "Deploy Bot (redacted-bot-id)",
  "id": "redacted-bot-id",
  "name": "Deploy Bot",
  "is_bot": true
}

=== human sender ===
sender.isBot (monitor build) = undefined  ->  ctxPayload.SenderIsBot = undefined
Sender (untrusted metadata):
{
  "label": "Alice (redacted-user-id)",
  "id": "redacted-user-id",
  "name": "Alice",
  "username": "alice@example.com"
}
~~~

A bot sender reaches the agent prompt with `is_bot: true` in the untrusted sender metadata; a human sender omits the field. The same bot and human behavior is also asserted by focused unit tests in `monitor.test.ts`.
target.author: masatohoshino
